context: Consider this, if you will, Lamentations chapter 3.
point_summary: God's faithfulness, affirmed throughout Scripture, is anchored in Lamentations 3 by setting the familiar passage within its context of Judah's captivity and Jerusalem's ruins.

context: remembering my affliction and my misery, the wormwood and the gall, my soul hath them still in remembrance and is humbled in me.
point_summary: Even in the ruins of Jerusalem, Jeremiah's remembrance of affliction gives way to hope grounded in mercies that are new every morning—declaring God's faithfulness independent of human faithfulness.

context: this I recall to my mind, therefore have I hope.
point_summary: Even amid the ruins of Jerusalem, Jeremiah's deliberate act of recalling God's mercies to mind becomes the ground of hope, illustrating that divine faithfulness is not contingent on human faithfulness.

context: It is of the Lord's mercies that we are not consumed, because His compassions fail not.
point_summary: Even amid the ruins of Jerusalem, Jeremiah's declaration that God's mercies are new every morning establishes that divine faithfulness is not contingent on human faithfulness.

context: They are new every morning, great is thy faithfulness.
point_summary: Even amid national ruin and personal anguish, God's mercies renew each morning—demonstrating that His faithfulness is not contingent on human faithfulness.

context: The Lord is my portion, saith my soul, therefore will I hope in Him.
point_summary: God's faithfulness, declared amid Jerusalem's ruins in Lamentations 3:24, is shown to be unconditional—not contingent on human faithfulness.

context: The Lord is good unto them that wait for Him, to the soul that seeketh Him.
point_summary: God's faithfulness to His people is not contingent on their faithfulness to Him, a truth drawn from Lamentations 3:25's surrounding context to contrast Abraham's fear-driven failure with God's unwavering character.

context: But there in verse number 23, they are new every morning, great is thy faithfulness.
point_summary: God's faithfulness to His people is not contingent on their faithfulness to Him, a truth drawn from Lamentations 3:23 to frame Abraham's failure in Genesis 20 against the backdrop of divine constancy.

context: I want you to take and look at the book of Lamentations just for a moment in the third chapter
point_summary: Lamentations 3:55, often overshadowed by the familiar mercy verses, shows Jeremiah calling on God's name from the lowest extremity — modeling the very act of prayer the sermon commends.

context: but I want to draw your attention this evening to verse 55 of Lamentations chapter 3. And here in this verse of Scripture, notice what Jeremiah says. He said, I called upon thy name, O Lord, out of the low dungeon.
point_summary: Even from the depths of a "low dungeon," God hears the cry of His people and draws near with the reassurance "fear not," demonstrating His accessibility in overwhelming circumstances.

context: You know, I think about the truth of verse 57 when the Bible says that Jeremiah says, in the day that I called, thou drewest near in the day that I called upon thee, thou saidst, fear not.
point_summary: God's nearness in answered prayer brings the reassurance "fear not," countering the fear that overwhelming circumstances can produce in His people.

context: But we find that as Jeremiah testifies in Lamentations 3, he says, I called and you drew near and you said to me, fear not.
point_summary: Jeremiah's cry from the low dungeon and God's answering nearness with "fear not" illustrate that God hears and draws close to His people in prayer, even in overwhelming circumstances.

context: 'Let us search and try our ways, and turn again unto the Lord.'
point_summary: Self-examination as a pathway to repentance is grounded in a biblical pattern, with Lamentations 3:40 cited as one of many calls to search one's ways and turn again to the Lord.

context: the statement of the prophet Jeremiah in Lamentations when he declares, great is thy faithfulness.
point_summary: God's faithfulness is not partial or developing but constant, dependable, and trustworthy, as Jeremiah's declaration in Lamentations anchors the sermon's third attribute of God.

context: Again, as Jeremiah proclaimed, they are new every morning, great is thy faithfulness in Lamentations 3 and verse number 25.
point_summary: God's faithfulness, declared by Jeremiah in Lamentations 3:25 as "great is thy faithfulness," means He is constant, dependable, and trustworthy—never becoming faithful, but always so by nature.

context: In Lamentations 3, in verse number 23.
point_summary: God's faithfulness, celebrated in Lamentations 3:23 and echoed in the hymn "Great Is Thy Faithfulness," is the very ground on which believers can hold their confession without wavering.

context: I want you to take and look at the book of Lamentations just for a moment in the third chapter, Lamentations in the third chapter.
point_summary: Lamentations 3:55, often overshadowed by the familiar mercy verses, shows Jeremiah crying out to God from the lowest extremity — modeling the very act of calling upon God's name in desperate circumstances.

context: But I want to draw your attention this evening to verse 55 of Lamentations chapter 3. And here in this verse of Scripture, notice what Jeremiah says. He said, I called upon thy name, O Lord, out of the low dungeon.
point_summary: Even from the lowest depths of despair, God hears the cry of his people and draws near with the assurance "fear not," demonstrating that he is a present help in trouble.

context: You know, I think about the truth of verse 57 when the Bible says that Jeremiah says, in the day that I called, thou drewest near in the day that I called upon thee, thou saidst, fear not.
point_summary: God's nearness in answered prayer brings the reassurance "fear not," countering the fear that overwhelming circumstances can produce in his people.

context: But we find that as Jeremiah testifies in Lamentations 3, he says, I called and you drew near and you said to me, fear not.
point_summary: Jeremiah's cry from the low dungeon and God's answering nearness with "fear not" illustrate that God hears and draws close to His people in their most overwhelming circumstances.

context: His mercies are new every morning
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: in the book of Lamentations, the third chapter, a very familiar passage of scripture for us, but we find that the third chapter of the book of Lamentations in verse number 21, here's what the writer says, this I recall to my mind, therefore have I hope.
point_summary: Recalling God's mercies to mind — as Jeremiah did amid Babylon's devastation — is the very act that produces hope, mirroring Moses' deliberate recollection of God's revealed character in Numbers 14.

context: It is of the Lord's mercies that we are not consumed, because His compassions fail not.
point_summary: Recalling God's mercies to mind — as Jeremiah did amid Babylonian devastation — is presented as the very act that sustains hope when circumstances seem to argue against it.

context: Where new every morning, great is thy faithfulness.
point_summary: God's mercies being "new every morning" is cited as the ground of hope that Jeremiah deliberately recalled to his mind amid lamentation, paralleling Moses' recollection of God's revealed character in Numbers 14.

context: Mine eye affecteth... my heart, because of all the daughters of my city.
point_summary: Seeing a need firsthand—what passes through the "eye gate"—affects the heart in a way that secondhand reports cannot, just as Nehemiah's night survey moved him beyond what he had merely been told.

context: Let me remind you of what it says in the book of Lamentations. If you want to turn there, we'll finish with this text of Scripture. Jeremiah, Lamentations. Lamentations chapter number 5. I'm just going to look at one verse of Scripture and we'll close this evening.
point_summary: Faithful transmission of God's eternal Word across generations grounds the call to intentional, disciplined engagement with the generation to come, since God alone spans every generation while human influence is finite.

context: Notice what Lamentations 5 and verse 19 says about our God. Again, the one who spans every generation, the one whose hope we're seeking to have our children set their hope in this God. It says in verse number 19 of Lamentations 5, thou, O Lord, remainest forever. Thy throne from generation to generation.
point_summary: God's eternal throne spanning every generation grounds the urgency to intentionally pass faith to children, since the Lord endures when human mentors and parents do not.

context: for these things I weep, mine eye, mine eye runneth down with water because the comforter that should relieve my soul is far from me. My children are desolate because the enemy prevailed.
point_summary: Jeremiah's lament over the destruction of Judah—"for these things I weep"—illustrates that godly sorrow expressed through tears is a legitimate and recurring biblical emotion.

context: mine eye affecteth my heart.
point_summary: Grief perceived through the eye reaches inward to move the heart, illustrating how tears are not merely physical but reflect a deep emotional and spiritual response to sorrow.

context: It's here that in Lamentations chapter 3,
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Notice what Jeremiah will say in verse 21. This I recall to my mind. Therefore, have I hope.
point_summary: In the depths of lament over Jerusalem's destruction, Jeremiah's deliberate act of recalling truth to mind becomes the turning point that produces hope.

context: this I recall to my mind. Therefore, have I hope.
point_summary: Deliberately recalling God's unfailing compassions to mind—as Jeremiah did amid Jerusalem's ruin—is presented as the active, chosen act that produces hope in the midst of lament.

context: It is of the Lord's mercies that we are not consumed because His compassions fail not.
point_summary: Recalling God's inexhaustible compassions—not consumed despite deserved judgment—is what produces hope in the midst of lament, grounding the call to trust God and pour out one's heart before Him.

context: They are new every morning. Great is thy faithfulness.
point_summary: God's mercies being new every morning means that recalling this truth to mind produces hope, comfort, and expectation even amid deep lament.

context: The Bible says this, it is of the Lord's mercies. It's of the Lord's mercies that we're not consumed.
point_summary: God's mercy and compassion are the sole reason believers are not consumed, and those compassions are continually renewed each morning, grounding confidence in His faithfulness.

context: It's of the Lord's mercies that we're not consumed. That we're not consumed because his compassions fail not.
point_summary: God's mercy and compassion are inexhaustible and continually renewed, so that without them humanity would be utterly consumed — a truth the preacher calls direct encouragement for those who serve a merciful God.

context: They are new every morning, great is thy faithfulness.
point_summary: God's daily renewal of compassion is the ground of his faithfulness, assuring believers that his mercies are not exhausted but freshly given each morning.

context: five, Lamentations, chapter number five, and Jeremiah and then Lamentations. And I'll be looking at the fifth chapter this evening, really looking at the last several verses of Lamentations, chapter number five.
point_summary: Written against the backdrop of Babylon's destruction of Jerusalem, Lamentations is Jeremiah's book of mourning — its very title meaning "to cry aloud" — over the judgment God brought upon Judah for her sin.

context: Thou, O Lord, remainest forever thy throne from generation to generation.
point_summary: God's eternality and unassailable sovereignty—his throne enduring from generation to generation, never to be conquered—form the theological foundation from which Jeremiah's closing prayer in Lamentations is launched.

context: Wherefore, dost thou forget us forever and forsake us so long time?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Turn thou us unto thee, O Lord, and we shall be turned. Renew our days as of old.
point_summary: Lamentations 5:21 is the sermon's focal verse, set within a closing prayer that acknowledges God's eternal, undefeatable reign as the foundation for Israel's plea to be restored.

context: But thou hast utterly rejected us. Thou art very wroth against us.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: thou remainest forever thy throne from generation to generation
point_summary: Jeremiah's prayer opens with recognition of God's eternality—that He remains forever, His throne unshaken from generation to generation, and can never be dethroned.

context: Wherefore, dost thou forget us forever and forsake us so long time?
point_summary: Verse 20's anguished question — "Wherefore dost thou forget us forever?" — is read as the voice of a suffering people expressing how prolonged adversity can make God's silence feel like abandonment.

context: But thou hast utterly rejected us. Thou art very wroth against us.
point_summary: Verse 22 closes Lamentations on a stark note of divine judgment, which the sermon identifies as Jeremiah's recognition that God's wrath fell on his people as a consequence of their sin.

context: turn thou us unto thee, O Lord, and we shall be turned, renew our days as of old.
point_summary: Lamentations 5:21 is read as a twofold prayer: a cry for God to initiate the turning of his straying people back to himself, and a plea for the renewal of former days—linked here to the need for revival.

context: the prayer of Jeremiah here in Lamentations 5 and verse 21
point_summary: Lamentations 5:21 is read as a twofold prayer: a plea for God to initiate the turning back of his people, and a cry for renewal of the former days of sweet fellowship and blessing.

context: turn thou us unto Thee, O Lord. And He says this, and we shall be turned. And then He says renew our days as of old.
point_summary: The prayer "turn thou us unto thee" in Lamentations 5:21 is presented as a desperate petition acknowledging that genuine return to God requires divine initiative, not merely human resolve.

context: Jeremiah in Lamentations, he says, turn thou us unto thee.
point_summary: Lamentations 5:21's cry "turn thou us unto thee" reflects that spiritual straying is the people's failure, not God's abandonment, requiring a return initiated by divine grace.

context: What a challenge from Lamentations chapter five.
point_summary: Jeremiah's closing plea in Lamentations 5 — "turn thou us unto thee, renew our days as of old" — is read as a prayer for restored communion with God, warning against the gradual drift that cost the Ephesian church its first love.

context: Turn thou us unto thee, O Lord, and we shall be turned. He asked for renewal as of the days of old.
point_summary: Revival begins with individual repentance and return to God, and Jeremiah's plea—"Turn thou us unto thee"—is presented as the pattern: personal renewal spreading outward to home, church, and land.

context: if you read in the book of Lamentations in the third chapter, he did just that. He called to God, and God answered, and God delivered, and God worked
point_summary: Jeremiah's experience in Lamentations 3 is cited as a concrete Old Testament example of prayer promise fulfilled—he called to God, and God answered and delivered.

context: you read the book of Lamentations and you find that Jeremiah specifically deals with the fact that he called and God answered
point_summary: Jeremiah's experience in Lamentations confirms the open invitation of James 1:5—that God hears and answers when his people actually call upon him.

context: Great is thy faithfulness
point_summary: God's faithfulness—celebrated in Lamentations 3:23—stands as the foundation and model for the human faithfulness required of those entrusted with ministry stewardship.

context: His mercies are new every morning
point_summary: God's delight in mercy—cited from Micah 7:18 and echoed in Lamentations 3:22—distinguishes the true God from false deities who are imagined as perpetually angry and unappeased.

context: the truth of Lamentations 3, great is thy faithfulness
point_summary: Even amid Judah's devastating judgment by Babylon, Lamentations 3 declares God's faithfulness—the truth that inspired the hymn "Great Is Thy Faithfulness" and anchors Paul's repeated assurances to the Thessalonians.

context: Lamentations chapter 1
point_summary: Lamentations is cited as direct biblical evidence that Jerusalem's ruin—broken walls, burned gates, captivity—was the consequence of sin, confirming Proverbs 14:34's principle that sin brings reproach upon a people.

context: Jerusalem hath grievously sinned. Therefore, she is removed. All that honored her despise her, because they have seen her nakedness
point_summary: Jerusalem's catastrophic fall — her walls broken, her gates burned, her people in reproach — is traced directly to her grievous sin, confirming that national judgment follows national unrighteousness.

context: Jerusalem
point_summary: Sin's reproach upon a nation is confirmed in Lamentations, where Jeremiah describes Jerusalem's disobedience and grievous sin, paralleling Nehemiah's own confession of corrupt dealing against God.
